message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 16 (29 todo)
Recent changes: The "enchantment 4 no surcharge" test passed immediately (>=5 threshold already in place); no implementation change in Green.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Keep all tests green. Attempt at least one refactoring or document why none is warranted (brief is fine — no code changed in Green).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 16 tests pass. Now I'll evaluate the Refactor phase.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase made no code change — the "enchantment 4 no surcharge" test passed immediately because the `>= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5) check was already in place. So I'll review the existing implementation for any naming or quality improvements warranted by the current full set of 16 passing tests.

Reviewing names:
- `highEnchantmentSurcharge`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_RATE` — clear, reveal intent. The threshold-5 / enchantment-4 boundary tests confirm `>=` semantics; the name `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` accurately describes this.
- `curseSurcharge`, `isCursed`, `itemSurcharges`, `sumOfBasePremiums` — all clear.
- `firstInsuranceSurchargeWhileCursedOnly` — accurate but flags a known temporary hack (documented in the comment).

No naming change is warranted by the new test.

## Step 2 & 3: Evaluate the actual refactoring candidate

The standout code-quality issue is the `firstInsuranceSurchargeWhileCursedOnly` temporary hack (lines 84-90). However, this is a **deliberate scaffolding** documented to be lifted out by the upcoming `policy-wide modifiers` tests (specifically "should add 10% first-insurance surcharge on the policy base premium" at line 207). Refactoring it now would require guessing the policy-wide design ahead of the test that drives it — a TDD violation (implementing ahead of tests).

Let me look for an improvement that is **driven by code currently under test** and does not anticipate future tests. Consider `itemBasePremium`:

content.content:
79	const highEnchantmentSurcharge = (item: QuoteItem): number =>
80	  (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD
81	    ? itemBasePremium(item) * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_RATE
82	    : 0;
83

content.text:
I found a small but genuine **Rule 2 (reveals intent)** improvement that is fully driven by the currently-passing tests and does not anticipate any future test.

In `highEnchantmentSurcharge`, the expression `item.enchantment ?? 0` defaults a missing enchantment to `0`. The intent — "an item with no enchantment is treated as enchantment level 0" — is implicit. The boundary tests just exercised (enchantment 4 → no surcharge, enchantment 5 → surcharge) make the enchantment-level concept central, so naming this value as an explaining variable better reveals intent.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass (`highEnchantmentSurcharge`)

```
(item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD
  ? itemBasePremium(item) * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_RATE : 0

- Constants:    1  (literal 0)                                        = 1
- Bindings:     4  (item, item.enchantment, HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD,
                    HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_RATE)                            = 4
- Invocations:  3  (?? , itemBasePremium(), *)                        = 6
- Conditionals: 2  (?? coalescing, ?: ternary)                       = 8
- Loops:        0                                                     = 0
- Assignments:  0                                                     = 0

Total Mass: 19
```

Now apply the refactoring — extract an `enchantmentLevel` explaining variable.

input.old_string:
const highEnchantmentSurcharge = (item: QuoteItem): number =>
  (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD
    ? itemBasePremium(item) * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_RATE
    : 0;

content.text:
All 16 tests still pass.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The expression now reads `enchantmentLevel(item) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`, plus a new named helper.

```
enchantmentLevel(item) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD
  ? itemBasePremium(item) * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_RATE : 0

- Constants:    0                                                     = 0
- Bindings:     3  (HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD, item, HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_RATE) = 3
- Invocations:  3  (enchantmentLevel(), itemBasePremium(), *)         = 6
- Conditionals: 1  (?: ternary)                                       = 4
Subtotal: 13

New helper: enchantmentLevel = item.enchantment ?? 0
- Constants:    1  (literal 0)                                        = 1
- Bindings:     2  (item, item.enchantment)                           = 2
- Invocations:  1  (??)                                               = 2
- Conditionals: 1  (?? coalescing)                                    = 4
Subtotal: 9

Total Mass: 22
Mass Change: 19 → 22 (Δ +3)
```

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied** — Extract explaining-variable helper `enchantmentLevel(item)`:
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): All 16 tests green before and after.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): The "missing enchantment means level 0" rule is now a named, reusable concept rather than an inline `?? 0`. The boundary tests just added (enchantment 4 vs 5) make the enchantment-level concept first-class, so naming it improves clarity exactly where the latest test focused.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): No new duplication; the helper is a single source of truth for "an item's effective enchantment level," ready for reuse without anticipating any specific future test.
- APP: Mass rose 19 → 22 (Δ +3). **Rule 2 trumps APP** — this is the documented, accepted tradeoff: a small mass increase buys a clearer, named domain concept.

**Refactoring NOT done (documented):** The `firstInsuranceSurchargeWhileCursedOnly` hack (lines 86-92) is the largest known wart, but it is deliberate scaffolding flagged to be replaced by the upcoming "10% first-insurance surcharge on the policy base premium" test. Refactoring it now would mean implementing the policy-wide design ahead of the test that drives it — a TDD violation. It is correctly left for that Red/Green cycle.

## Step 7: Report Completion

```
Refactor Phase Complete:
Refactoring: Extracted explaining-variable helper enchantmentLevel(item)
             (Rule 2 — reveals intent), clarifying the "no enchantment = level 0"
             rule exercised by the enchantment 4/5 boundary tests.
Mass Change: 19 → 22 (Δ +3; Rule 2 deliberately trumps APP)
Tests: 16 passing, 29 todo — all green.
```

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_07-22-49_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

Proceeding to the next test: `should apply both curse and high-enchantment surcharges to a cursed sword with enchantment 5` (line 201).
